skill.name: reasoning-inductive
skill.description: Extract patterns and generalizations from multiple observations. Use when detecting recurring themes, building predictive rules, or identifying systemic behaviors from accumulated data. Produces validated patterns with confidence bounds and exception handling.
skill.summary: Extract repeatable patterns from observations and turn them into confidence-scored generalizations.
skill.author: BellaBe

security_audit.summary: All static findings were reviewed against SKILL.md and were false positives from Markdown syntax or reasoning terminology. The skill is a single Markdown guide with no executable scripts, shell commands, network access, filesystem operations, credential access, or prompt-injection language.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Flagged as Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections correspond to fenced Markdown examples and output contracts. They are documentation blocks, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown code fences containing type signatures, YAML examples, and sample outputs. No executable source file, command call, or user input execution path exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reasoning Terms Flagged as Security Keywords
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance detections are ordinary reasoning text, headings, and examples. No hash function, encryption primitive, host discovery, or environment enumeration code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text describes pattern types, confidence bounds, and example business observations. I found no cryptographic implementation, reconnaissance command, or operational security behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams often see repeated events but struggle to turn them into reliable rules. This skill structures observations, detects patterns, and reports confidence bounds with exceptions.
actual_capabilities: Collects multiple observations into a structured analysis frame.
Separates frequency, correlation, sequence, cluster, trend, and threshold patterns.
Forms candidate rules from repeated evidence and stated applicability limits.
Adds confidence bounds based on sample size, strength, consistency, and recency.
Identifies exceptions and conditions that may invalidate a rule.
Suggests actions, monitoring needs, and further data collection.
limitations: It does not prove causation from correlation alone.
Small samples produce tentative guidance rather than strong rules.
Results depend on the quality and consistency of supplied observations.
It does not execute automated scans or access external data by itself.

use_cases.title: Find Product Usage Patterns
use_cases.description: Analyze repeated customer feedback, support tickets, or feature usage notes to identify reliable product behavior patterns.

use_cases.title: Review Sales Outcomes
use_cases.description: Compare multiple deals to find recurring stalls, conversion signals, exception cases, and forecasting rules.

use_cases.title: Synthesize Research Observations
use_cases.description: Turn interview notes, experiment logs, or field observations into evidence-backed themes with confidence levels.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Basic Patterns
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis with a small set of observations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use inductive reasoning on these observations. Identify repeated patterns, weak signals, and exceptions. Keep confidence levels conservative.

prompt_templates.title: Build Candidate Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these recurring events and form candidate rules. Include evidence, applicability, confidence bounds, and conditions that could invalidate each rule.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Business Assumption
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a current hypothesis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use inductive reasoning to test this assumption against the supplied observations. Separate supporting evidence, contrary evidence, exceptions, and next validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Pattern Strength Across Segments
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis across multiple groups or time periods.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the observations by segment and time period. Identify stable patterns, drift, confounders, confidence bounds, and rules suitable for action.

output_examples.input: Twelve enterprise sales deals with stage history, deal size, duration, and outcome.
output_examples.output: The analysis identifies legal review as the most common stall point, gives a moderate confidence rule for large deals, and lists exceptions for existing customers.

output_examples.input: Twenty-four marketing content records with topic, format, channel, visits, and demo conversions.
output_examples.output: The result highlights case studies and long technical articles as stronger performers, with confidence ranges and suggested follow-up tests.

output_examples.input: A collection of customer interviews about onboarding delays.
output_examples.output: The response groups recurring blockers, marks which patterns are actionable, and recommends additional observations for low-confidence themes.

best_practices: Provide at least five comparable observations before asking for rules.
Include negative cases and exceptions, not only successful examples.
Ask for confidence bounds and invalidation conditions before acting.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to explain a single isolated event.
Do not treat correlation as proof of cause without further validation.
Do not hide missing data or inconsistent observation formats.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It turns repeated observations into structured patterns, candidate rules, confidence bounds, and exception lists.

faq.question: How much data should I provide?
faq.answer: Use at least five observations for exploration. Use more than twenty observations for stronger generalizations.

faq.question: Can it prove cause and effect?
faq.answer: No. It can suggest likely patterns, but causal claims need separate testing or evidence.

faq.question: What makes the output reliable?
faq.answer: Reliability improves when observations are comparable, recent, complete, and include both confirming and contrary cases.

faq.question: Does it run automated scans?
faq.answer: No. It describes an analysis workflow and output structure, but it does not execute commands or read files by itself.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: It is listed for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that support Markdown skill instructions.
